questions.to_find: The correct statements for A (incrementing/decrementing character counts) and B (the return value / termination check).

understand.plain: An anagram is a rearrangement of the characters of one string to form another. The function uses a frequency array of size 128 (covering all ASCII characters). It increments the count for each character in string 'a' and decrements it for each character in 'b'. If both strings are anagrams, all counts should be zero at the end. Statement A must increment for 'a' and decrement for 'b'. Statement B must return 1 (true) only when both strings end simultaneously, i.e., they are the same length AND all counts are zero.

keywords.explain: A string formed by rearranging all letters of another string.
An array indexed by character value that stores how many times each character appears.
A 7-bit character encoding with 128 code points, so count[128] covers every possible character.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='180' viewBox='0 0 500 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Anagram Check: count[] after processing</text><text x='30' y='65' class='sub ink'>a = "abc",  b = "bca"</text><text x='30' y='90' class='sub ink'>count[a]: +1 for a, +1 for b, +1 for c</text><text x='30' y='110' class='sub ink'>count[b]: -1 for b, -1 for c, -1 for a</text><rect x='30' y='125' width='120' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='90' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>All counts = 0</text><text x='200' y='145' class='sub mut'>=> Anagram: return 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing frequency array counts incrementing for string 'a' characters and decrementing for string 'b' characters, ending with all zeros when strings are anagrams.

given.aim: Identify statement A (character counting logic) and statement B (the final return value).

terms.meaning: Frequency difference array: positive means 'a' has more of that char, negative means 'b' has more.
terms.connects: After the while loop, all entries must be 0 for an anagram.

terms.meaning: The body of the while loop that updates the count array for both strings.
terms.connects: Ensures a net-zero count for each character if both strings are anagrams.

terms.meaning: The return value when all counts are 0 — must be 1 (true), AND both strings must be fully consumed (same length check).
terms.example: !(a[j]) && !(b[j]) means both strings ended at the same position.
terms.connects: If lengths differ, one string has characters remaining, so the while loop exits early and counts won't all be zero anyway; returning 1 is correct only when both end together.

given.plan: For A: to cancel out characters, increment count for 'a' and decrement for 'b'. For B: return 1 (anagram confirmed) only when both strings terminated at the same index, i.e., !(a[j]) && !(b[j]). The for-loop already handles returning 0 if any count is non-zero.

steps.title: Determine statement A
steps.apply: For anagram detection, each character in 'a' increments its slot and each character in 'b' decrements it. If the strings are anagrams, every slot returns to 0. Option B uses ++ for both — that would never produce zeros from 'b'. Options C uses -- for 'a' and ++ for 'b' — reversed logic. So A must be: count[a[j]]++; count[b[j]]--;
steps.note: The direction matters: 'a' adds, 'b' subtracts. Any other combination breaks the cancellation.

steps.title: Determine statement B
steps.apply: After the for-loop confirms all counts are zero, the function returns B. B must be 1 when both strings are equally consumed (same length). !(a[j]) is true when a[j] == '\0', i.e., string 'a' ended. Similarly for b. Using && means BOTH ended — same length, same characters => true anagram. Using || would return 1 even if only one string ended, which is wrong for unequal lengths. So B = (!(a[j]) && !(b[j])).
steps.note: The while loop exits when either string ends; if only one ends, the counts may still all be zero but strings have different lengths, so we must verify both ended.

solution.result: A: count[a[j]]++; count[b[j]]--;   B: (!(a[j]) && !(b[j])) — Option A is correct.

questions.formula_note: Character frequency cancellation: increment for string 'a', decrement for string 'b'; all counts zero and both strings fully consumed implies anagram.

questions.to_find: The integer value printed by f(20, 1).

understand.plain: The recursive function f(n, k) halves n at each step and doubles k. If n is odd it adds k to the result; if n is even it subtracts k. When n reaches 0 it returns 0. Tracing from f(20, 1) step by step gives the final integer output.

keywords.explain: A function that calls itself with modified arguments until a base case is reached.
keywords.example: f(20,1) calls f(10,2), which calls f(5,4), etc.

keywords.explain: The remainder when n is divided by 2; 0 means even, 1 means odd.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='240' viewBox='0 0 520 240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='220' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Recursion Trace for f(20, 1)</text><text x='30' y='60' class='sub ink'>f(20, 1)  — even  => f(10, 2) - 1</text><text x='30' y='82' class='sub ink'>f(10, 2)  — even  => f(5,  4) - 2</text><text x='30' y='104' class='sub ink'>f(5,  4)  — odd   => f(2,  8) + 4</text><text x='30' y='126' class='sub ink'>f(2,  8)  — even  => f(1, 16) - 8</text><text x='30' y='148' class='sub ink'>f(1, 16)  — odd   => f(0, 32) + 16 = 16</text><line x1='30' y1='158' x2='490' y2='158' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='30' y='175' class='sub mut'>Unwinding: 16 - 8 = 8  |  8 + 4 = 12  |  12 - 2 = 10  |  10 - 1 = 9</text><rect x='380' y='188' width='110' height='32' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='435' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Answer: 9</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursion trace table showing f(20,1) expanding down to base case f(0,32)=0, then unwinding with + and - operations to yield 9.

given.aim: Trace the recursive calls of f(20, 1) to compute the output.

terms.meaning: Recursive function: returns 0 if n=0; adds k if n is odd; subtracts k if n is even; always recurses with (n/2, 2*k).
terms.connects: Each level's contribution is determined by the parity of n at that level.
Drives the recursion toward the base case n == 0.

terms.meaning: The weight k doubles at each level of recursion.
terms.connects: Higher-order bits of n receive higher weight — the function computes the binary-to-decimal value of n.

given.plan: Trace each call recording n, k, and whether even/odd. Accumulate results on the way back up.

steps.title: Expand recursion (top-down)
steps.formula_raw: f(n,k) = f(n/2, 2k) +/- k  depending on n%2
steps.note: The sign pattern (+/-) follows the odd/even parity of n at each level.

steps.title: Unwind (bottom-up) to compute result
steps.formula_raw: result = sum of signed k values
steps.note: 20 in binary is 10100. The function effectively computes the signed binary value based on bit positions, giving 9.

solution.result: The output printed is 9. Correct option is C.

questions.formula_note: Recursive evaluation with binary bit-weight interpretation: each bit of n contributes +k (if bit=1) or -k (if bit=0) with k doubling each level, computing a signed binary-weighted sum.

questions.to_find: The correct base-case condition E that makes the function compute C(m, n) correctly.

understand.plain: C(m, n) is the binomial coefficient (combinations). The recursive formula is C(m, n) = C(m-1, n) + C(m-1, n-1). The base cases are: C(m, 0) = 1 (choosing 0 items is always 1 way) and C(m, m) = 1 (choosing all items is always 1 way). The condition E must capture both base cases so the recursion terminates correctly.

keywords.explain: The number of ways to choose n items from m items, denoted C(m,n) or mCn.
keywords.example: C(4, 2) = 6 means there are 6 ways to choose 2 from 4.

keywords.explain: The stopping condition in a recursion where the result is known directly without further recursive calls.
C(m, n) = C(m-1, n) + C(m-1, n-1), the recurrence used to build Pascal's triangle.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='200' viewBox='0 0 500 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Base Cases for C(m, n)</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='200' height='45' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>n == 0</text><text x='130' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C(m, 0) = 1  for all m</text><rect x='270' y='55' width='200' height='45' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='370' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>n == m</text><text x='370' y='93' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C(m, m) = 1  for all m</text><text x='250' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Both base cases return 1 => combine with ||</text><rect x='140' y='150' width='220' height='32' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='171' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>E = (n == 0) || (n == m)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two base cases for Pascal's triangle / binomial coefficient: C(m,0)=1 when n==0, and C(m,m)=1 when n==m, combined with logical OR.

given.aim: Find the base-case condition E such that func(m, n) correctly computes C(m, n).

terms.meaning: Pascal's identity: the recurrence relation for binomial coefficients.
terms.connects: This is exactly what the else branch computes.

terms.meaning: Choosing 0 items from any group has exactly 1 way (the empty selection).
terms.connects: Covered by the n==0 part of E.

terms.meaning: Choosing all m items from m has exactly 1 way.
terms.connects: Covered by the n==m part of E.

given.plan: Identify both base cases of C(m,n): n=0 and n=m. Combine them with OR since either alone should trigger the return 1.

steps.title: Identify base cases of C(m, n)
steps.apply:
The binomial coefficient has two natural base cases:
1) When n = 0: there is exactly 1 way to choose nothing => C(m, 0) = 1.
2) When n = m: there is exactly 1 way to choose everything => C(m, m) = 1.
Both return 1, so the condition E must return 1 in either case.
steps.note: The constraints say m >= 1 and m >= n, so m==0 is never reached; options involving m==0 are incorrect.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. (n==0) only: misses the n==m base case — recursion would go negative (n-1 when n=m could cause wrong results or infinite recursion).
B. (n==0) && (m==0): both conditions together — m is never 0 per constraints, so this never triggers. Wrong.
C. (n==0) || (n==m): captures both base cases. Correct.
D. (n==m) && (m==0): m is never 0, so never triggers. Wrong.
steps.note: Without the n==m base case, func(m-1, m-1) would call func(m-2, m-2) and func(m-2, m-1) — the second call has n > m which is illegal.

solution.result: E = (n == 0) || (n == m). Correct option is C.

questions.formula_note: Binomial coefficient via Pascal's identity C(m,n) = C(m-1,n) + C(m-1,n-1) with base cases C(m,0)=1 and C(m,m)=1.
